content.problemStatement:
Maui had an undirected connected graph G=(V,E)G = (V, E)G=(V,E), where VVV = {v1,v2,…,vNv_1, v_2, \ldots, v_Nv1​,v2​,…,vN​} and EEE is the edge set. He also had a list of positive non-zero integers a1,a2,…,aNa_1, a_2, \ldots ,a_Na1​,a2​,…,aN​. He was bored of his usual adventures and decided to build a new directed graph G′=(U,F)G' = (U, F)G′=(U,F). UUU = {u1,u2,…,uNu_1, u_2, \ldots, u_Nu1​,u2​,…,uN​} and (ui,uj)∈F(u_i, u_j) \in F(ui​,uj​)∈F if and only if there is a walk of length exactly aia_iai​ from viv_ivi​ to vjv_jvj​ in GGG. Note that this walk could repeat edges. He then noticed that the new directed graph G′G'G′ had two vertices uiu_iui​ and uju_juj​ , such that there was no path from uiu_iui​ to uju_juj​ in G′G'G′. Note that there could be more such pairs of vertices.

But now, Maui has lost his list of integers, and has only the graph GGG. Help him by constructing any list which would satisfy the property mentioned, or state that it cannot be done.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains two integers: N,MN, MN,M, which represent the number of nodes and edges in GGG respectively.
The i-th of the next MMM lines contains two numbers iii and jjj, which signify that the undirected edge (vi,vj)(v_i, v_j)(vi​,vj​) exists in GGG.


Output Format

If a list of integers which satisfies the property exists, print "YES" in the first line and the NNN integers in order in the second line. Each of the integers must satisfy 1≤ai≤1091 \leq a_i \leq 10^91≤ai​≤109.
If it does not exist, print "NO".
constraints: The input graph will be connected.
There will be no multi-edges or self-loops.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains two integers: N and M, which represent the number of nodes and edges in the graph G respectively. The i-th of the next M lines contains two numbers i and j, which signify that the undirected edge (vi, vj) exists in G.
content.outputFormat: If a list of integers which satisfies the property exists, print 'YES' in the first line and the N integers in order in the second line. Each of the integers must satisfy 1 ≤ ai ≤ 10^9. If it does not exist, print 'NO'.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
In our output, a1=2, and so there will be a direct edge from u1 to u2 if and only if there is a walk of length 2 between v1 and v2. But there isn't any such walk. But there is a walk of length 2 between v1 and v3, and so there is a direct edge from u1 to u3 in G'. Similarly you can check that the edge set of G' is {(u1,u3),(u3,u1),(u2,u1),(u2,u3)}. Now notice that there is no path from u1 to u2 in G'. Hence this is a valid output.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a graph traversal algorithm such as Breadth-First Search (BFS) or Depth-First Search (DFS) to find the shortest path between each pair of nodes in the graph G. Then, we can construct the directed graph G' based on the given condition. Finally, we can check if there exists a pair of nodes in G' that are not connected by a path.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2 * M) — because in the worst case, we need to traverse the graph for each pair of nodes.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) — because we need to store the adjacency matrix of the graph G and the directed graph G'.
